title: Equality Act 2010
desc: Consolidates and replaces previous UK anti-discrimination legislation including the Sex Discrimination Act 1975, Race Relations Act 1976, and Disability Discrimination Act 1995. Defines nine protected characteristics and prohibits direct discrimination, indirect discrimination, harassment, and victimisation in the workplace and beyond. Enforced by the Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC).

sections.title: The protected characteristics
sections.summary: Defines the nine protected characteristics to which equality law applies throughout the Act.
sections.text:
The following characteristics are protected characteristics—

age; disability; gender reassignment; marriage and civil partnership; pregnancy and maternity; race; religion or belief; sex; sexual orientation.
refs: s.5 EqA 2010 (Age)
s.6 EqA 2010 (Disability)
s.7 EqA 2010 (Gender Reassignment)
s.9 EqA 2010 (Race)
s.10 EqA 2010 (Religion or Belief)
s.11 EqA 2010 (Sex)
s.12 EqA 2010 (Sexual Orientation)
EHRC Employment Statutory Code of Practice

sections.title: Disability
sections.summary: A person has the protected characteristic of disability if they have a physical or mental impairment that has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on their ability to carry out normal day-to-day activities.
sections.text:
(1) A person (P) has a disability if—
(a) P has a physical or mental impairment, and
(b) the impairment has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on P's ability to carry out normal day-to-day activities.

(2) A reference to a disabled person is a reference to a person who has a disability.

(4) This Act applies in relation to a person who has had a disability as it applies in relation to a person who has the disability.

(5) A Minister of State may issue guidance about matters to be taken into account in deciding any question for the purposes of subsection (1).

subs.text: Schedule 1 makes further provision about what is or is not to be treated as a disability. Certain conditions are always treated as disabilities (cancer, HIV infection, multiple sclerosis, a certified visual impairment). Certain conditions are excluded (addiction to alcohol, tobacco or drugs not prescribed by a medical practitioner; tendency to set fires; paedophilia; exhibitionism; voyeurism; hay fever unless it aggravates another condition).

refs: EqA 2010 Schedule 1 (Supplementary Provision)
Equality Act 2010 (Disability) Regulations 2010
Goodwin v Patent Office [1999] (Normal Day-to-Day Activities)
J v DLA Piper [2010] (Mental Health — Substantial and Long-Term)
s.15 EqA 2010 (Discrimination Arising from Disability)
s.20 EqA 2010 (Reasonable Adjustments)

sections.title: Race
sections.summary: Race includes colour, nationality, and ethnic or national origins. Includes Gypsies, Travellers, and Jews as distinct ethnic groups.
refs: Mandla v Dowell Lee [1983] AC 548 (Ethnic Group Definition)
Dawkins v Department of the Environment [1993] (Rastafarians)
s.13 EqA 2010 (Direct Discrimination)

sections.title: Religion or belief
sections.summary: Religion means any religion including a lack of religion. Belief means any religious or philosophical belief including a lack of belief. Must be genuinely held and worthy of respect in a democratic society.
sections.text:
(1) Religion means any religion and a reference to religion includes a reference to a lack of religion.

(2) Belief means any religious or philosophical belief and a reference to belief includes a reference to a lack of belief.
refs: Grainger plc v Nicholson [2010] (Philosophical Belief — Climate Change)
Forstater v CGD Europe [2021] (Gender-Critical Beliefs)
Mackereth v DWP [2022] (Gender-Critical Beliefs in Workplace)

sections.title: Direct discrimination
sections.summary: A person discriminates against another if they treat that other person less favourably than they treat or would treat others because of a protected characteristic.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) discriminates against another (B) if, because of a protected characteristic, A treats B less favourably than A treats or would treat others.

(2) If the protected characteristic is age, A does not discriminate against B if A can show A's treatment of B to be a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

(3) If the protected characteristic is disability, A also discriminates against B if A treats a person (C) who does not have the disability more favourably than A treats B, and the reason for the less favourable treatment is that B has the disability.

(5) If the protected characteristic is race, less favourable treatment includes segregating B from others.

(6) If the protected characteristic is sex, less favourable treatment of a woman includes less favourable treatment of her because she is breast-feeding.
refs: James v Eastleigh Borough Council [1990] (But For Test)
Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police v Khan [2001] (Comparator)
s.14 EqA 2010 (Combined Discrimination)
s.23 EqA 2010 (Comparators)

sections.title: Discrimination arising from disability
sections.summary: An employer discriminates against a disabled person if they treat them unfavourably because of something arising in consequence of their disability, and cannot show justification.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) discriminates against a disabled person (B) if—
(a) A treats B unfavourably because of something arising in consequence of B's disability, and
(b) A cannot show that the treatment is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

(2) Subsection (1) does not apply if A shows that A did not know, and could not reasonably have been expected to know, that B had the disability.
refs: Burnip v Birmingham CC [2012] (Arising in Consequence)
City of York Council v Grosset [2018] EWCA Civ 1105 (Knowledge of Disability)
s.20 EqA 2010 (Reasonable Adjustments)
Gallop v Newport City Council [2013] (Knowledge — Medical Information)

sections.title: Indirect discrimination
sections.summary: Applying a provision, criterion, or practice that disadvantages persons sharing a protected characteristic is indirect discrimination unless it is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) discriminates against another (B) if A applies to B a provision, criterion or practice which is discriminatory in relation to a relevant protected characteristic of B's.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), a provision, criterion or practice is discriminatory in relation to a relevant protected characteristic of B's if—
(a) A applies, or would apply, it to persons with whom B does not share the characteristic,
(b) it puts, or would put, persons with whom B shares the characteristic at a particular disadvantage when compared with persons with whom B does not share it,
(c) it puts, or would put, B at that disadvantage, and
(d) A cannot show it to be a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.
refs: Homer v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2012] UKSC 15 (PCP Definition)
Essop v Home Office [2017] UKSC 27 (No Need to Explain Why Disadvantage)
Mba v Merton LBC [2014] (Proportionality)

sections.title: Duty to make adjustments
sections.summary: Where a provision, criterion, or practice puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage, the employer is under a duty to take reasonable steps to avoid the disadvantage.
sections.text:
(2) The duty comprises the following three requirements.

(3) The first requirement is a requirement, where a provision, criterion or practice of A's puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage in relation to a relevant matter in comparison with persons who are not disabled, to take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to avoid the disadvantage.

(4) The second requirement is a requirement, where a physical feature puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage, to take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to avoid the disadvantage.

(5) The third requirement is a requirement, where a disabled person would, but for the provision of an auxiliary aid, be put at a substantial disadvantage, to take such steps as it is reasonable to have to take to provide the auxiliary aid.
refs: s.21 EqA 2010 (Failure to Comply — Discrimination)
EqA 2010 Schedule 8 (Work: Reasonable Adjustments)
Archibald v Fife Council [2004] UKHL 32 (Reasonable Adjustments — Redeployment)
EHRC Employment Code of Practice Chapter 6

sections.title: Harassment
sections.summary: Harassment occurs where a person engages in unwanted conduct related to a protected characteristic that violates dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) harasses another (B) if—
(a) A engages in unwanted conduct related to a relevant protected characteristic, and
(b) the conduct has the purpose or effect of—
(i) violating B's dignity, or
(ii) creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment for B.

(2) A also harasses B if A engages in unwanted conduct of a sexual nature, and the conduct has the purpose or effect mentioned in subsection (1)(b).

(4) In deciding whether conduct has the effect referred to in subsection (1)(b), each of the following must be taken into account—
(a) the perception of B;
(b) the other circumstances of the case;
(c) whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.
refs: s.40 EqA 2010 (Employer Liability for Third-Party Harassment — reinstated by ERA 2025 s.94)
ERA 2025 s.94 (Third-Party Harassment — Not Yet In Force)
EHRC Technical Guidance on Sexual Harassment

versions.text: s.26 as originally enacted included subsection (3) which provided for harassment where A or another person engages in unwanted conduct of a sexual nature or related to gender reassignment or sex, and because of B's rejection of or submission to the conduct, A treats B less favourably. The original s.26 operated alongside s.40(2)-(4) which provided employer liability for third-party harassment.
versions.policyRationale: Consolidated the harassment definitions from the SDA 1975, RRA 1976, and DDA 1995 into a single unified definition with three limbs: (1) unwanted conduct related to a protected characteristic; (2) unwanted conduct of a sexual nature; (3) less favourable treatment for rejecting or submitting to conduct.

versions.text: s.26 remains in force as enacted. However, the repeal of s.40(2)-(4) by ERRA 2013 s.65 removed the employer liability for third-party harassment that had operated alongside s.26. Following Unite the Union v Nailard [2018] EWCA Civ 1203, s.26 cannot be read as imposing liability on employers for third-party harassment absent the repealed s.40 provisions.
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.65 (repeal of s.40(2)-(4))
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition government repealed the third-party harassment provisions on grounds they imposed 'an unnecessary burden on business'. The Court of Appeal in Nailard confirmed that without s.40(2)-(4), the remaining harassment provisions could not be read as creating employer liability for third-party acts.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 reinstates employer liability for third-party harassment across all protected characteristics (not just sexual harassment) via new provisions in s.40. The Worker Protection Act 2023 had already inserted s.40A (preventive duty for sexual harassment, in force 26 October 2024). ERA 2025 strengthens 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' and extends third-party liability beyond sexual harassment.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — transforms employer exposure for customer-facing and service industry workplaces.

sections.keyPrinciple: Harassment is assessed by both purpose and effect. The perception of the complainant, the circumstances of the case, and the reasonableness of the effect must all be considered.

sections.title: Victimisation
sections.summary: Victimisation occurs where a person is treated badly because they have made or supported a complaint (a protected act) under equality law.
refs: St Helens Metropolitan Borough Council v Derbyshire [2007] UKHL 16 (Victimisation)

sections.title: Employees and applicants — prohibited conduct
sections.summary: Employer must not discriminate against or harass employees or job applicants in relation to any aspect of employment including recruitment, terms, promotion, and dismissal.
refs: s.13 EqA 2010 (Direct Discrimination)
s.19 EqA 2010 (Indirect Discrimination)
s.26 EqA 2010 (Harassment)
s.27 EqA 2010 (Victimisation)
s.109 EqA 2010 (Liability of Employer for Acts of Others)

sections.title: Enquiries about disability and health
sections.summary: Employer must not ask candidates about disability or health before making a job offer, except in very limited circumstances.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) to whom an application for work is made must not ask about the health of the applicant (B)—
(a) before offering work to B, or
(b) where A is not in a position to offer work to B, before including B in a pool of applicants from whom A intends to select a person to whom to offer work.

(5) Subsection (1) does not apply if the question asked is for the purpose of—
(a) establishing whether B will be able to comply with a requirement to undergo an assessment,
(b) establishing whether B will be able to carry out a function that is intrinsic to the work concerned,
(c) monitoring diversity in the range of persons applying to A for work.
refs: s.6 EqA 2010 (Disability)
s.20 EqA 2010 (Reasonable Adjustments)
EHRC Guidance on Pre-Employment Health Questions

sections.title: Equal work
sections.summary: Work is equal where it is like work, rated as equivalent under a job evaluation study, or found to be of equal value.
refs: s.69 EqA 2010 (Material Factor Defence)
Enderby v Frenchay Health Authority [1994] (Equal Value)

sections.title: Defence of material factor
sections.summary: An employer may defeat an equal pay claim by showing that the difference in terms is attributable to a material factor that does not involve treating the claimant less favourably because of their sex.
sections.text:
(1) The sex equality clause in A's terms has no effect in relation to a difference between A's terms and B's terms if the responsible person shows that the difference is because of a material factor reliance on which—
(a) does not involve treating A less favourably because of A's sex than the responsible person treats B, and
(b) if the factor is within subsection (2), is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

(2) A factor is within this subsection if A shows that, as a result of the factor, A and persons of the same sex doing work equal to A's are put at a particular disadvantage when compared with persons of the opposite sex doing work equal to A's.
refs: Glasgow City Council v Marshall [2000] (Material Factor — General Principles)
Enderby v Frenchay [1994] (Indirect Discrimination and Material Factor)

sections.title: Discussions about pay
sections.summary: Terms preventing workers from disclosing pay to colleagues for equal pay purposes are unenforceable. ERA 2025 s.97 extends this more broadly.
sections.text:
(1) A term of a person's work that purports to prevent or restrict the person (P) from disclosing or seeking to disclose information about the terms of P's work is unenforceable against P in so far as P makes or seeks to make a relevant pay disclosure.

(3) "Relevant pay disclosure" means a disclosure made for the purpose of enabling the person who makes it, or the person to whom it is made, to find out whether or to what extent there is, in relation to the work in question, a connection between pay and having (or not having) a particular protected characteristic.
refs: ERA 2025 s.97 (Broader Pay Secrecy Prohibition — Not Yet In Force)
s.64 EqA 2010 (Equal Pay)

sections.title: Liability of employers and principals
sections.summary: Employers are vicariously liable for acts of discrimination, harassment, and victimisation carried out by their employees in the course of employment, unless they can show they took all reasonable steps to prevent the act.
sections.text:
(1) Anything done by a person (A) in the course of A's employment must be treated as also done by the employer.

(3) It does not matter whether the employer knew of or approved of the act.

(4) In proceedings against A in respect of an act alleged to have been done by A's employee, it is a defence for A to show that A took all reasonable steps to prevent the employee—
(a) from doing that thing, or
(b) from doing anything of that description.
refs: Lister v Hesley Hall [2002] UKHL 22 (Course of Employment)
Jones v Tower Boot Co Ltd [1997] (Course of Employment)
Canniffe v East Riding of Yorkshire Council [2000] (Reasonable Steps Defence)
s.110 EqA 2010 (Liability of Employees)

sections.title: Liability of employees
sections.summary: An employee who carries out an act of discrimination or harassment is personally liable, even if the employer is also liable.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) contravenes this section if—
(a) A is an employee or agent,
(b) A does something which, by virtue of section 109(1) or (2), is treated as having been done by A's employer or principal, and
(c) the doing of that thing by A amounts to a contravention of this Act by the employer or principal.

(2) It does not matter whether, in any proceedings, the employer or principal has been found to have contravened this Act.
refs: s.109 EqA 2010 (Employer Vicarious Liability)
Gilbank v Miles [2006] (Personal Liability)

sections.title: Employment tribunals — time limits
sections.summary: Complaints of discrimination in work must be presented within 3 months of the act complained of (subject to ACAS early conciliation). Tribunal has discretion to extend on a just and equitable basis.
sections.text:
(1) Proceedings on a complaint within section 120 may not be brought after the end of—
(a) the period of 3 months starting with the date of the act to which the complaint relates, or
(b) such other period as the employment tribunal thinks just and equitable.

(2) In relation to an act that extends over a period, the reference in subsection (1)(a) to the date of the act is a reference to the last day of the period.
refs: Employment Tribunals Act 1996 (ACAS Early Conciliation)
Matuszowicz v Kingston upon Hull City Council [2009] (Continuing Acts)
Robertson v Bexley Community Centre [2003] (Just and Equitable Extension)

sections.title: Employment tribunals — remedies
sections.summary: Where a complaint is upheld, tribunal may make a declaration, award compensation (uncapped), and make a recommendation.
refs: s.119 EqA 2010 (County Court Remedies)
Chagger v Abbey National plc [2010] (Uncapped Compensation)
Prison Service v Johnson [1997] (Injury to Feelings — Vento Bands)

parliamentaryOrigin.sponsoringMinister: Harriet Harman MP (Minister for Women and Equality, Leader of the House of Commons)

readings.keyPoints: Equality Bill introduced by Harriet Harman. The Bill aimed to consolidate over 116 separate pieces of equality legislation including nine major Acts accumulated since the 1970s into a single coherent framework.
readings.significance: The largest consolidation and reform of equality law ever undertaken in the UK. The Bill was a key Labour manifesto commitment from 2005.

readings.keyPoints: Carry-over motion agreed to allow the Bill to span the 2008-09 and 2009-10 parliamentary sessions.
readings.significance: The Bill's complexity required carry-over — unusual for government legislation and reflecting the scale of the consolidation exercise.

readings.hansardRef: HC Public Bill Committee, Equality Bill, Session 2008-09
readings.keyPoints: Extensive Public Bill Committee proceedings. Key debates on the socio-economic duty (cl.1), combined discrimination (cl.14), pre-employment health questions, positive action, and the public sector equality duty. Significant third-party evidence received from EHRC, TUC, CBI, Disability Rights Commission, and others.
readings.significance: The Committee heard extensive evidence on the practical operation of the consolidated framework. Several key provisions were debated and amended.

readings.keyPoints: Bill passed the House of Commons. Key debates on the public sector equality duty, socio-economic duty, and positive action provisions.
readings.significance: The Bill left the Commons substantially as introduced, with the core framework of nine protected characteristics and four types of prohibited conduct intact.

readings.keyPoints: Baroness Royall of Blaisdon led for the government. Cross-party tribute paid to Harriet Harman's role in driving the Bill. Lords scrutiny focused on religious exemptions, disability provisions, the socio-economic duty, and combined discrimination. The Conservative spokesman described the Bill as having been 'a long time coming' but acknowledged its consolidation purpose.
readings.significance: The Lords Second Reading debate revealed the depth of parliamentary scrutiny the Bill would receive, with particular focus on religious liberty, disability rights, and the scope of the public sector equality duty.

readings.keyPoints: Extensive Lords Committee stage. Key amendments debated on faith-based exemptions for religious organisations, the caste discrimination provision (s.9(5)), disability definition, and age discrimination in services.
readings.significance: Lords Committee stage produced significant amendments, particularly on religious exemptions and the scope of protection for disabled people.

readings.keyPoints: Lords Report stage. Further debate on religious exemptions and the scope of the public sector equality duty.
readings.significance: Several Lords amendments were agreed, requiring the Commons to consider them before Royal Assent.

readings.keyPoints: Bill passed the House of Lords.
readings.significance: The Bill left the Lords with amendments that required consideration by the Commons.

readings.keyPoints: The Commons considered and accepted Lords amendments. The parliamentary process was completed shortly after 11pm on 6 April 2010, just days before Parliament was dissolved for the 2010 General Election.
readings.significance: The Bill was passed in the final days of the Brown government. Several provisions (notably the socio-economic duty s.1 and combined discrimination s.14) were enacted but not brought into force by the incoming Coalition government.

readings.keyPoints: Equality Act 2010 (c.15) received Royal Assent on 8 April 2010. The majority of its provisions came into force on 1 October 2010.
readings.significance: The Act consolidated over 116 pieces of equality legislation. It remains the governing framework for all anti-discrimination law in employment, services, education, and public functions in Great Britain.

significantAmendments.text: Public bodies to have due regard to reducing inequalities of outcome resulting from socio-economic disadvantage.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.1 but never commenced by the Coalition government in England. Commenced in Scotland (Fairer Scotland Duty, 2018) and Wales (2021).
significantAmendments.significance: Described by one cabinet member as 'socialism in one clause'. The non-commencement by the Coalition illustrated the political contestability of the equality framework.

significantAmendments.text: Provision for claims of direct discrimination on the basis of a combination of two protected characteristics.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.14 but never commenced. The Coalition government decided not to bring it into force, citing regulatory burden.
significantAmendments.significance: Combined discrimination remains a dormant provision of the Act.

significantAmendments.text: Employer liability for harassment of employees by third parties (customers, clients) where the employer knew of previous incidents and failed to act.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.40(2)-(4) with a 'three strikes' rule. Subsequently repealed by ERRA 2013 s.65. Partially reinstated by Worker Protection Act 2023 s.40A (preventive duty). ERA 2025 extends further.
significantAmendments.significance: The third-party harassment provisions have had the most turbulent legislative history of any provision in the Act.

keyDebates.ministerialStatement: Baroness Royall stated that the Bill brought together nine protected characteristics into a single coherent framework, providing clarity for employers, service providers, and individuals about their rights and responsibilities.
keyDebates.significance: The consolidation of previously separate strands of discrimination law into a unified protected characteristics framework was the defining architectural achievement of the Act.

keyDebates.hansardRef: HC Public Bill Committee, Equality Bill
keyDebates.ministerialStatement: The Solicitor-General explained that the third-party harassment provision (then cl.40(2)-(4)) was included to address situations where employees were subjected to harassment by customers, clients or other third parties, and their employer failed to take reasonable steps to prevent it after being made aware of the problem.
keyDebates.significance: The original parliamentary intent was to protect employees from third-party harassment. The subsequent repeal (2013) and partial reinstatement (2023/2025) of this provision is one of the most significant legislative oscillations in UK employment law.

precedingDocuments.title: Discrimination Law Review: A Framework for Fairness
precedingDocuments.publisher: Department for Communities and Local Government
precedingDocuments.significance: The foundational policy document setting out the case for a single Equality Act to replace the fragmented existing legislation.

precedingDocuments.title: The Equalities Review: Fairness and Freedom
precedingDocuments.significance: Independent review that recommended a single Equality Act and a simplified public sector equality duty.

precedingDocuments.title: Framework for a Fairer Future — The Equality Bill
precedingDocuments.significance: The White Paper that preceded the Bill, setting out the government's proposals for the Equality Act.

precedingDocuments.title: Sex Discrimination Act 1975
precedingDocuments.significance: One of the principal statutes replaced by EqA 2010. Prohibited sex discrimination in employment and services.

precedingDocuments.title: Race Relations Act 1976
precedingDocuments.significance: Prohibited race discrimination. Replaced by the EqA 2010 unified framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Disability Discrimination Act 1995
precedingDocuments.significance: Established the duty to make reasonable adjustments and prohibited disability discrimination. Replaced by EqA 2010.

precedingDocuments.title: Equal Pay Act 1970
precedingDocuments.significance: The original equal pay statute. Replaced by EqA 2010 Part 5 Chapter 3 (Equality of Terms).

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2006
precedingDocuments.significance: Created the Equality and Human Rights Commission and extended protection from discrimination on grounds of religion or belief and sexual orientation. Partially replaced by EqA 2010.

explanatoryNotes.note: Comprehensive Explanatory Notes accompany the EqA 2010, explaining each Part and section in detail. The Notes were published alongside the Bill and updated at each parliamentary stage.

selectCommitteeReports.committee: House of Commons Women and Equalities Committee
selectCommitteeReports.title: Sexual harassment in the workplace (HC 725, 2017-19)
selectCommitteeReports.significance: Recommended reinstating employer liability for third-party harassment and introducing a preventive duty on employers. Led to the Worker Protection Act 2023.

selectCommitteeReports.committee: House of Commons Work and Pensions Committee
selectCommitteeReports.title: The Equality Bill: how disability equality fits within a Single Equality Act (HC 158-I, 2008-09)
selectCommitteeReports.significance: Scrutinised the disability provisions of the Bill, ensuring the duty to make reasonable adjustments was preserved and strengthened in the consolidated framework.

leadingCases.name: Essop v Home Office; Naeem v Secretary of State for Justice [2017] UKSC 27
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that in an indirect discrimination claim under s.19, the claimant does not need to explain why a provision, criterion, or practice puts persons sharing a protected characteristic at a particular disadvantage. The statistical disparity is sufficient.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the causation standard for indirect discrimination under s.19 EqA 2010. Significantly lowered the evidentiary burden on claimants.

leadingCases.name: Homer v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2012] UKSC 15
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that the definition of 'provision, criterion or practice' in s.19 is broad and includes any formal or informal practice applied by the employer, even where it is not a written policy.
leadingCases.significance: Established the broad scope of indirect discrimination claims and confirmed that the PCP concept is widely drawn.

leadingCases.name: Archibald v Fife Council [2004] UKHL 32
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the duty to make reasonable adjustments under disability discrimination law may require an employer to treat a disabled person more favourably than others, including by transferring them to a vacant position without competitive interview.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the scope of the duty to make reasonable adjustments. Confirmed that the duty is anticipatory and may require positive discrimination in favour of disabled employees.

leadingCases.name: Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that Uber drivers were 'workers' for the purposes of employment legislation including the EqA 2010, entitling them to equality protections.
leadingCases.significance: Extended the reach of the EqA 2010 to gig economy workers by confirming their status as workers entitled to equality protections.

leadingCases.name: Forstater v CGD Europe [2021] UKEAT 0105/20
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that gender-critical beliefs (the belief that sex is immutable and should not be conflated with gender identity) are protected as a philosophical belief under s.10 EqA 2010, satisfying the Grainger criteria.
leadingCases.significance: A landmark ruling on the scope of philosophical belief protection under s.10. The case attracted significant public and media attention and influenced subsequent litigation on gender-critical beliefs in the workplace.

leadingCases.name: For Women Scotland Ltd v Scottish Ministers [2025] UKSC 16
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held unanimously that for the purposes of the Equality Act 2010, 'sex' in s.11 refers to biological sex as recorded at birth, irrespective of whether a person holds a Gender Recognition Certificate.
leadingCases.significance: A defining ruling on the meaning of 'sex' in the EqA 2010. Confirmed that the protected characteristic of sex is determined by biological sex, with implications for single-sex services, spaces, and employment practices.

leadingCases.name: City of York Council v Grosset [2018] EWCA Civ 1105
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the question of whether something 'arises in consequence of' a disability under s.15 is an objective question. The employer's knowledge of the disability is relevant to the s.15(2) defence but does not determine whether the unfavourable treatment arose in consequence of the disability.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on s.15 discrimination arising from disability. Clarified the relationship between the causation test and the employer's knowledge defence.

leadingCases.name: Vento v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2003] ICR 318
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal established three bands for injury to feelings awards in discrimination cases: a lower band for less serious cases, a middle band for serious cases that do not merit the highest award, and an upper band for the most serious cases.
leadingCases.significance: The Vento bands remain the governing framework for injury to feelings compensation in discrimination claims. The bands are updated annually by Presidential Guidance.

leadingCases.name: James v Eastleigh Borough Council [1990] 2 AC 751
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the test for direct discrimination is objective — the 'but for' test. If the complainant would not have received the less favourable treatment 'but for' their protected characteristic, that is sufficient. The discriminator's motive or intention is irrelevant.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'but for' test for direct discrimination, which remains the governing standard under s.13 EqA 2010.

leadingCases.name: Mandla v Dowell Lee [1983] 2 AC 548
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords established the criteria for determining whether a group constitutes an 'ethnic group' for the purposes of race discrimination law: a long shared history, a cultural tradition of its own, a common geographical origin or descent, a common language, a common literature, a common religion, and being a minority or oppressed group.
leadingCases.significance: The foundational authority on the definition of 'ethnic origins' under s.9 EqA 2010. Established that Sikhs are an ethnic group. The criteria continue to govern ethnic group determinations.

leadingCases.name: Grainger plc v Nicholson [2010] ICR 360
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established five criteria for a belief to qualify as a 'philosophical belief' under s.10: (1) genuinely held; (2) not merely an opinion or viewpoint; (3) relates to a weighty and substantial aspect of human life and behaviour; (4) attains a certain level of cogency, seriousness, cohesion and importance; (5) worthy of respect in a democratic society.
leadingCases.significance: The Grainger criteria remain the governing test for philosophical belief protection under s.10 EqA 2010. Applied in Forstater, Mackereth, and numerous subsequent cases.

leadingCases.name: Unite the Union v Nailard [2018] EWCA Civ 1203
leadingCases.heldText: Following the repeal of s.40(2)-(4) by ERRA 2013, the remaining provisions of the EqA 2010 cannot be read as creating employer liability for third-party harassment. For an employer to be liable, their failure to act must itself be motivated by the protected characteristic.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the gap in protection created by the ERRA 2013 repeal. Directly influenced the Worker Protection Act 2023 and ERA 2025 reforms to reinstate third-party harassment liability.

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — third-party harassment liability (s.40, anticipated Oct 2026)
ERA 2025 s.97 — broader pay secrecy prohibition
For Women Scotland v Scottish Ministers [2025] UKSC 16 — 'sex' means biological sex
